episode_meta.title: Jim Friedlich, David Chivers & Matt Boggie: How the Lenfest AI Collaborative placed AI engineers in 10 newsrooms

ai_tools: Dewey (Philadelphia Inquirer custom archive search tool, RAG-based)
Notebook LM (Google, used by product managers at the Inquirer)
Microsoft AI tools (via Microsoft partnership and software credits)
Slack integrations with AI (Chicago Public Media weather and traffic automation)
OAuth-based LLM authentication (emerging publisher content access method)

use_cases.description: Dewey: full archive search system using RAG that allows reporters to query the entire Philadelphia Inquirer archive (back to 1978) via a chatbot interface, with nightly refresh of newly published stories
use_cases.tool_used: Dewey (custom RAG application built on LLM)
use_cases.outcome: Deep archive research that previously took up to a week now saves up to two days (nearly 40% time savings); deployed to newsroom and also used by ad sales and marketing teams

use_cases.description: Incorporating photography into the Dewey archive search, scanning and indexing published photos plus outtakes from original shoots to make visual assets searchable and reusable
use_cases.outcome: Stretch goal now underway; aims to make all photos including unpublished alternatives accessible for reporting, apps, and products

use_cases.description: Advertising prospecting tool that analyzes the local market, categories, and geography to identify advertisers the sales team has never called on or that resemble existing advertisers
use_cases.tool_used: Custom AI tool (built during two-to-three week sprint)
use_cases.outcome: Sold additional advertising fairly quickly out of the box; demonstrates self-funding potential of AI investments

use_cases.description: Data vault project to make decades of collected local information (own reporting, other sources, public data) accessible via AI, with potential reporting and marketing services applications
use_cases.tool_used: AI-powered data access layer with fine-tuning for news applications
use_cases.outcome: Fellow Shawnee Yu began work and was able to incorporate freshness-solving code from Philadelphia Inquirer's fellow, accelerating development

use_cases.description: Automated weather reports generated in the voice of WBEZ on-air talent via Slack integration, replacing the hour or two previously spent gathering information from disparate sources
use_cases.outcome: Recording-ready scripts produced on demand; also replicated for traffic reports

use_cases.description: Monitoring and summarizing public meetings to find signal in the noise, enabling coverage of local government that newsrooms had abandoned due to cost
use_cases.tool_used: AI processing of public records and recordings
use_cases.outcome: Described as restoring a coverage capability lost two decades ago; work underway across the cohort

use_cases.description: Ad sales team using Dewey to research coverage history of potential clients before meetings
use_cases.outcome: Sales reps can quickly assess how the Inquirer has covered a particular business in the past to bring context to client conversations

use_cases.description: Marketing team using Dewey to identify trends in a reporter's coverage for campaign building and promotional messaging
use_cases.outcome: Enables data-driven marketing campaigns aligned with editorial strengths

use_cases.description: Product managers using Notebook LM to ingest project charters, requirements documents, decision matrices, and meeting notes for instant retrieval of project history and decisions
use_cases.outcome: Can quickly answer questions like 'when did we decide not to make this feature?' or 'why did we choose this vendor?' from source documents

use_cases.description: Converting screenshots and JPEGs of presentation slides into data tables using ChatGPT for manipulation in Excel
use_cases.outcome: Each instance saves approximately an hour; estimated cumulative savings of at least a week

key_claims.claim: Each of the 10 newsrooms in the collaborative received $250,000 per year to embed an AI engineer who reports directly to the newsroom, not to Lenfest, Microsoft, or OpenAI
key_claims.speaker: Jim Friedlich

key_claims.claim: The fellowship grants are platform-agnostic; newsrooms are not required to use OpenAI or Microsoft products exclusively or at all
key_claims.speaker: Jim Friedlich

key_claims.claim: For all 10 organizations in the cohort, this was the first AI engineering resource they had ever had on their teams
key_claims.speaker: David Chivers

key_claims.claim: Deep archive research at the Inquirer that previously took up to a week now saves up to two days with Dewey, a nearly 40% time improvement
key_claims.speaker: Matt Boggie

key_claims.claim: The Dewey prototype that was expected to take 24 months to build had working code after a two-week hackathon with Microsoft's MADA team, and was released broadly to the newsroom about four months later
key_claims.speaker: David Chivers / Matt Boggie

key_claims.claim: Google search becoming Google Answers has caused major news organizations to see their top-of-funnel traffic drop 20-30%, consistently across major news organizations
key_claims.speaker: Jim Friedlich

key_claims.claim: News as a field is a small opportunity for OpenAI and Microsoft relative to infrastructure and other industries, so they will not spend time fine-tuning tools specifically for news
key_claims.speaker: Matt Boggie

key_claims.claim: The use of AI in the news field is dramatically undercapitalized; these were the first AI engineers in each of these respective news organizations, some of which are quite large
key_claims.speaker: Jim Friedlich

key_claims.claim: Each fellow effectively gives an organization the equivalent of 'a person and a half' because fellows across the cohort check each other's code, swap code, and accelerate each other's work
key_claims.speaker: David Chivers

challenges.description: LLMs are not good at understanding chronology of information; they may surface a disaster from 20 years ago instead of one from two weeks ago when querying archives (the 'freshness' problem)

LLMs perform poorly on very specific needle-in-a-haystack queries ('did we ever mention X person?') and exhaustive list queries ('give me every time we talked about Y'), requiring supporting applications around them
Reporter concerns about job replacement and function replacement when AI tools are introduced, requiring hands-on experience to overcome
Google search shifting to Google Answers has reduced top-of-funnel traffic to news sites by 20-30%, threatening the existing business model
Sustainability of AI engineering support after the two-year fellowship program ends; newsrooms need to fund these positions on their own
Trust in AI platforms given history of failed tech partnerships (Facebook Pivot to Video, Facebook Journalism Project) that 'came and went'
Getting started with AI is a real problem for newsrooms that lack existing engineering skills on their teams
AI fellows can be isolated as the only person 'speaking their nouns and verbs' in their organization, requiring community of practice to avoid loneliness and burnout
AI interfaces becoming a primary gateway for news may erode publisher brands and reduce direct audience relationships

quotes.text: There is no better profession or skill set than a journalist to use these tools because you're really good at asking questions, which is basically the prompting piece of it. You're naturally skeptical because you want to verify the information that you're getting and you want to double check your sources.
quotes.speaker: Matt Boggie
quotes.topic: Journalists as natural AI power users
quotes.pedagogical_value: Reframes AI adoption as a natural extension of core journalistic skills rather than an alien technology

quotes.text: No one is coming to us and saying, we want you to go in this direction because it's good for our platform. Instead, we're being approached and saying, we have some really interesting technologies that we think might be useful to you. Let's work together and figure out ways that they can be applied to your everyday.
quotes.speaker: Matt Boggie
quotes.topic: How the Lenfest AI Collaborative differs from past platform partnerships like pivot to video
quotes.pedagogical_value: Articulates a concrete distinction between extractive platform relationships and co-development partnerships

quotes.text: I don't think the question should be like, how do we save the article or how do we drive consumption on site. I think the question should be, what is the job that journalism needs to do for people and how can AI help us deliver that in new ways?
quotes.speaker: David Chivers
quotes.topic: Reframing the AI disruption question for news organizations
quotes.pedagogical_value: Shifts the strategic conversation from defending legacy formats to reimagining journalism's core mission

quotes.text: The more they got to use the tools, they realized this helps support me really well. And it also does a terrible job at my job if I leave it alone.
quotes.speaker: Matt Boggie
quotes.topic: Overcoming reporter fears about AI job replacement
quotes.pedagogical_value: Captures the experiential shift from AI anxiety to understanding AI as augmentation, not replacement

quotes.text: AI and the fact that we have these public records and recordings all of a sudden creates an opportunity that we let go of two decades ago.
quotes.speaker: David Chivers
quotes.topic: AI restoring lost local news coverage capacity like public meetings
quotes.pedagogical_value: Illustrates how AI can restore journalism functions abandoned due to economics rather than just optimize existing ones

quotes.text: One, it's not a huge opportunity in comparison to infrastructure and other things, two, they don't have the expertise to do it. And what we're learning with AI is there is this sort of conjunction between the human-machine collaboration where the machines can do a lot of things, but they don't have this sort of domain expertise.
quotes.speaker: Matt Boggie
quotes.topic: Why newsrooms must build their own AI capabilities rather than waiting for tech companies to build for them
quotes.pedagogical_value: Explains the structural reason newsrooms cannot outsource AI development to platform companies

recommendations.advice: Get started with commercially available AI tools (many have free tiers), experiment with use cases, then be loud internally about marketing the new capability to find unexpected pockets of interest and creativity
recommendations.speaker: Matt Boggie
recommendations.target_audience: small newsrooms without dedicated AI staff

recommendations.advice: Develop AI policy and guidelines as an important early step; Lenfest Institute has published a guide to developing newsroom AI policy at lenfestinstitute.org
recommendations.speaker: David Chivers

recommendations.advice: Set up internal communication channels (e.g., Slack channels called 'AI Enthusiast' or 'AI Ambassador') to encourage staff to share what they're seeing and trying with AI
recommendations.speaker: David Chivers

recommendations.advice: Accept that your teams are already using AI more than you realize and put guidelines in place with a clear conversation about transparency, especially when AI makes its way into public-facing content
recommendations.speaker: David Chivers

recommendations.advice: As CEO or editor-in-chief, send the signal that AI is really important, embrace people's efforts, reward them, and celebrate them; as head of technology, find like-minded people and evangelize
recommendations.speaker: Jim Friedlich

recommendations.advice: Train reporters and staff on how LLMs actually learn and what they know, including what types of queries they are bad at, to build realistic expectations and trust
recommendations.speaker: Matt Boggie

recommendations.advice: Focus AI development on business sustainability use cases so the tools can pay for themselves when grant funding ends (e.g., advertising prospecting, operational efficiency)
recommendations.speaker: Jim Friedlich
recommendations.target_audience: newsrooms receiving AI grants or considering AI investment

recommendations.advice: Explore emerging content access mechanisms including OAuth-authenticated LLM access, short summaries instead of full article text to LLMs, and content payment clearinghouses to protect publisher value
recommendations.speaker: Matt Boggie
recommendations.target_audience: news publishers concerned about AI platforms consuming their content
